resources.string: Your data is safe on this device. Try again in a moment.
A private, science-informed path to quit porn — built around you.
No account. No cloud. Everything stays on this device.
Optional — choose \'Prefer not to say\' to skip.
How long has porn felt like a problem?
There\'s no judgment here — this shapes your plan.
On average, how often do you watch?
An honest answer sets the right starting intensity.
What most often leads you to it?
Pick the strongest one — you can track others later.
Late nights and urges are closely linked.
What\'s the longest you\'ve gone without it?
Past attempts are data, not failures.
What are you here to do?
Why does this matter to you?
Choose all that apply — this becomes your \'why\'.
There\'s no wrong answer — your plan meets you where you are.
Step down at your own pace
Built from your answers. It adapts as you go.
You can revisit and adjust this anytime from Settings.
Two minutes each day to log mood, urges, and wins. Consistency beats intensity.
Practice the breathing and delay tools once while calm, so they\'re familiar when it counts.
Turn the reasons you chose into one sentence you\'ll see when it matters most.
One clear target to start. Reaching it rewires what feels possible.
Build the identity, not just the streak
You\'re not \'trying to stop\' — you\'re becoming someone who doesn\'t watch. Small daily proof compounds.
Ninety days gives your brain\'s reward system a genuine reset. Week by week, not all at once.
Define clear step-downs — fewer days, tighter limits — so progress is measurable, not vague.
Daily habits hide in routines. Spot the exact times and places, then plan around them.
Blockers, app limits, grayscale after dark — make the easy path the healthy one.
Use breathing as your stress reset
When stress spikes, ninety seconds of slow breathing beats willpower. It\'s built into your SOS button.
Five go-to activities you can start in under a minute. Boredom needs an answer ready.
Loneliness is the trigger, so connection is the tool: one real interaction a day, even a message.
Track when you\'re most drained this week. Fatigue urges are usually a sleep problem in disguise.
Unfollow and mute the accounts that start the spiral. The urge often begins three scrolls earlier.
Before acting on an urge, write one line about what you\'re actually feeling. Naming it breaks the loop.
\'If an urge hits, then I open the toolkit.\' Deciding in advance removes the hardest step.
Phone out of the bedroom, or locked down after a set hour. Late-night urges lose their easiest doorway.
Feet on the floor, phone stays down for the first 20 minutes. Momentum before temptation.
A repeatable wind-down — shower, walk, music — that carries you through the risky hours.
A short walk or stretch at your danger hour. Change your state before the urge chooses for you.
Thirty screen-light minutes before bed. Calmer nights mean fewer late-night battles.
Pick one consistent lights-out time. A stable schedule quietly removes dozens of risky hours a week.
Motivation grows from evidence. Commit to the check-in and one tool — nothing more yet.
You\'re starting strong — bank it. Set up your environment and routines this week while it\'s easy.
You\'ve gone long stretches before. Write down what happened at the end — that\'s where your plan needs armor.
Writing for two minutes can take an urge\'s power away.

string-array.name: daily_quotes
string-array.item: Every urge you outlast teaches your brain a new ending.
You are not starting over. You are starting stronger.
The craving is loud because it is losing.
Progress is quiet. Keep making it.
One honest day is worth more than a perfect week imagined.
Discomfort is the feeling of rewiring.
Urges peak, then pass. You only have to outlast the peak.
Small promises kept build unshakable trust — with yourself.
You can\'t control the wave, but you\'re learning to surf.
Today\'s boredom is tomorrow\'s peace, in disguise.
Healing isn\'t linear. It\'s still healing.
Every clean morning is proof, not luck.
You are allowed to find this hard and do it anyway.
Your attention is the most valuable thing you own. Reclaim it.
The streak is a side effect. The real win is who you\'re becoming.
The number grows on its own. Your job is just today.
Freedom feels like this: choosing, not being chosen for.
A slip is a data point, not a verdict.
Rest is part of recovery, not a break from it.
What you water grows. Stop watering the weeds.
Cravings are old echoes. You\'re writing new sound.
Real strength is asking for the tools before you need them.
Tonight\'s sleep is tomorrow\'s willpower.
Your hardest days so far all ended. This one will too.
Quiet consistency beats loud intensity.
You noticed the urge. That awareness is already a win.
Two minutes of writing can defuse an hour of wanting.
Be the person your first milestone believes you are.

resources.string: Nothing scheduled today — rest is part of the plan.
Small daily habits are what carry a recovery. Start with one.
Its history will be deleted too. This can\'t be undone.
Allow notifications in system settings to receive this reminder.
Gentle reminders for the habits you schedule.
A small step keeps the momentum.
A perfect week — every scheduled habit done. That\'s momentum.
\'%1$s\' could use some love. Try attaching it to a routine you already have.
Habit completion is up %1$d points on last week.
A lighter week than last — that\'s okay. Restart with just one habit.
Your average mood is trending up this week.
Urges are running quieter than last week.
%1$d-day check-in streak — the picture is getting clearer.
%1$d days to your milestone. Stay close to your plan.
Log a few days of habits and check-ins and your weekly insights will appear here.
You\'re safe. Let\'s ride this out.
Urges and anxiety peak, then pass. Pick a tool.
Slow the body first — the mind follows.
Come back to the room through your senses.
Catch the thought. Answer it kindly.
The reasons you chose, in your own words.
These are self-help tools, not a substitute for professional support.

string-array.name: grounding_steps
string-array.item: Look around. Name five things you can see — anything at all.
Notice four things you can feel. Your feet on the floor. Fabric. Air.
Listen. Find three sounds, near or far.
Two things you can smell — or two smells you like.
One thing you can taste. Take a slow breath.
You\'re here. The wave is already passing.

string-array.name: muscle_steps
string-array.item: Sit or stand comfortably. Take one slow breath.
Make fists. Squeeze for five seconds — then let go completely.
Shrug your shoulders up to your ears. Hold… and drop them.
Scrunch your face gently. Hold… and soften everything.
Tense your stomach. Hold… release, and breathe out long.
Press your feet into the floor. Hold… and let your legs go heavy.
Scan head to toe. Wherever you find tension, breathe into it and let it drop.

string-array.name: distraction_ideas
string-array.item: Put on one song you actually love and just listen — nothing else.
Tidy one small surface: a desk, a shelf, a drawer.
Step outside, even just to your doorway, and notice the temperature.
Text someone something you appreciate about them.
Make a cup of tea or coffee, slowly, and pay attention to it.
Look up something you\'ve been mildly curious about lately.
Stretch for two minutes — nothing fancy, just move.
Reorganize one small thing on your phone: photos, apps, notes.

resources.string: Complete onboarding to see your reasons here.
Catch a thought and reframe it.
The automatic thought, word for word.
A kinder, truer way to see it
What would you tell a friend in the same spot?
See your streak at a glance.
This resets your current streak, but nothing is erased — your history and best streak are always saved. You\'ll be guided through what comes next.
You\'re already doing better than yesterday.
Four days in. This is becoming real.
Halfway to a full week back.
One more day to a full week.
A week ago you chose to restart.
Two weeks of showing up for yourself.
Three weeks — new habits are taking root.
A full month since you chose to begin again.
Every day back is proof, not luck.
One relapse doesn\'t erase your progress.
Your history is still yours. Let\'s figure out what comes next.
This stays between you and this app.
Naming it is part of moving through it.
Recognizing the pattern is most of the fight.
Most relapses trace back to one of these. Recognizing the pattern is most of the fight.
Unstructured time is one of the most common triggers. A pre-decided replacement activity removes the decision fatigue in the moment.
Stress narrows focus toward fast relief. A short breathing reset lowers the physical intensity before it turns into an urge.
When the trigger is disconnection, the tool is connection — even one honest message to someone you trust changes the moment.
The spiral often starts a few scrolls before the urge itself. Notice which feeds tend to lead there.
Late hours combine tiredness, privacy, and low willpower — and lying awake unable to sleep makes the pull even stronger. A consistent wind-down routine removes much of the risk before it starts.
A short list to help reset — check off what you do.
Decide what you\'re doing for the next hour
Put your phone away and lie down
Day 1 starts the moment you close this. That\'s all it takes.
You proved you can reach %1$d days.
You chose to come back. That\'s the hardest part.
Three days in and still going.
A full week since you restarted.
You\'ve now gone longer than ever before.
A one-time purchase. Removes ads everywhere in the app, permanently — no subscription.
Thanks for supporting Clarity — every ad is gone, for good.
Your purchase is being confirmed by Google Play. This can take a moment.
Your purchase is processing — Premium will activate automatically once it\'s confirmed.
Purchases aren\'t available right now. Please try again later.
I made it through this urge.
A quick reset when you have almost no time.
A full minute to let the spike pass.
The longer reset, when you can spare it.

Turn the water cold for the last 30–60 seconds of your shower — or take a short one entirely cold. It genuinely interrupts an urge by giving your body something immediate to respond to. Come back here when you\'re done.
Change your surroundings, on the move.
Leave the room you\'re in. Doesn\'t need to be far — around the block, down the hall, out the door. Distance from the moment is often enough.
Do as many push-ups as you can manage right now — modify however you need to (knees, wall, whatever works). The point isn\'t the number, it\'s giving the energy somewhere else to go.
Get a full glass of water and drink it slowly. Small physical resets like this genuinely help interrupt an urge — and it costs you nothing to try.
You don\'t have to explain why.
Call or message someone you trust — you don\'t need to explain what\'s going on. \"Can you talk for a bit?\" is enough. Connection is one of the most effective interruptions there is.
Get it out of your head.
Your own reasons, in your own words.
A few things to try instead.
The first day is the loudest. You showed up anyway.
Three days in, and the pattern is already breaking.
Reach a 7-day streak — a full week.
A week is proof, not luck.
Reach a 14-day streak — two weeks strong.
Two weeks of choosing yourself, every single day.
Reach a 21-day streak — three weeks in.
Three weeks in, and a habit is quietly forming underneath.
Reach a 30-day streak — a full month.
A month. Whoever you were on day one, you\'re not that person anymore.
Fifty days of practice most people never even start.
Triple digits. This is who you are now.
Reach a 365-day streak — a full year.
A year. You didn\'t just survive it — you built something.
Start a new recovery run after a setback.
You didn\'t disappear. You came back — that\'s the whole thing.
Start a new recovery run five times.
Five times back on your feet. That\'s resilience with a track record.
Complete a daily check-in before noon.
Starting the day honest with yourself sets the tone for everything after.
Five entries in — you\'re learning what your own mind sounds like.
Use the Emergency Toolkit on 3 days in a row.
Three days of reaching for a tool instead of an urge — that\'s a new instinct forming.
Short, practical reads. No account, no tracking — just here when you want them.
It\'s a wave, not a command — and waves always pass.

An urge feels like an order you have to obey right now. It isn't. It's a wave of sensation and thought that rises, peaks, and falls — usually within 15 to 20 minutes, whether you act on it or not.

The mistake most people make is treating the urge as an emergency that has to be resolved immediately. That belief is what gives it its power. The urge itself is just a signal passing through your body.

You don't have to fight it or make it disappear. You just have to outlast it. Every time you do, the next wave gets a little easier to recognize for what it is.
A simple way to ride out a craving instead of fighting it.

Urge surfing is a technique borrowed from mindfulness-based relapse prevention. Instead of pushing an urge away or giving in to it, you observe it — the way a surfer rides a wave instead of trying to stop the ocean.

When an urge hits, try this: name what you're feeling, out loud or in your head ("this is an urge"). Notice where it shows up in your body — a tight chest, restless hands, racing thoughts. Breathe slowly and stay with the sensation for a minute without acting on it or trying to force it away.

The urge will crest and start to fade. It almost always does. Each time you surf one out instead of reacting to it, you're proving to yourself that the wave is survivable — and that proof is worth more than any amount of willpower.
Relying on grit in the moment sets you up to blame yourself later.

White-knuckling through urges works sometimes — until it doesn't. Willpower is a limited resource that gets used up by stress, poor sleep, and ordinary decision-making, so the moment you need it most is often the moment you have the least of it left.

That's not a character flaw. It's how self-control works for everyone. Treating every urge as a fresh willpower test, with no other plan, quietly stacks the odds against you no matter how motivated you are.

A better approach is to reduce how often you need willpower in the first place: fewer triggers within reach, a plan already decided before the urge shows up, and tools you can reach for on autopilot. Discipline still matters — it's just not meant to carry the whole load by itself.
Hungry, Angry, Lonely, Tired — most urges trace back to one of these.

H.A.L.T. is a quick checklist used across a lot of recovery work: Hungry, Angry, Lonely, Tired. It isn't scientific law, but for most people, urges cluster around these four states far more than random chance would suggest.

When an urge hits, running through the list takes ten seconds and often explains a lot: skipped a meal, stewing over something from earlier, haven't talked to anyone all day, running on five hours of sleep. Naming the real state underneath the urge makes it easier to address the actual problem instead of reaching for the same old response to everything.

Sometimes the fix is as unglamorous as eating something and going to bed early. That's not a consolation prize — for a lot of urges, it's the entire solution.
Cue, routine, reward — and why deleting one piece isn\'t enough.

Every habit runs on a loop: a cue triggers it, a routine plays out, and a reward reinforces it so the loop repeats. This one runs on the same loop any habit does — which is actually good news, because it means it follows rules that are well understood and changeable.

Most people try to break a habit by fighting the routine step in the moment, which is the hardest place to intervene. It's far easier to change the cues that start the loop, and to give yourself a different routine that still delivers some form of the reward — relief, distraction, comfort — the old one did.

This is why the tools in this app aren't about gritting your teeth through the routine step. They're about interrupting the loop earlier and redirecting it somewhere else entirely.
A habit rarely leaves an empty space behind — fill it on purpose.

Simply trying to stop a habit, with nothing planned to take its place, tends to leave a gap that the old habit is very good at refilling. The urge doesn't know it's supposed to disappear; it just knows there used to be something to do here.

Think about what the habit was actually giving you — stress relief, a break from boredom, a way to fall asleep, a moment of control — and find something that gives you a piece of that without the cost. It doesn't need to be a perfect substitute on day one.

Over time, the new routine becomes the automatic one, and the old cue stops pulling as hard. That's the whole idea behind a tool like the Emergency Toolkit: not a punishment, but somewhere else for the loop to go.
The easiest willpower is the kind you never have to use.

Environment shapes behavior more than most people give it credit for. If a trigger is one tap away at 11pm, you're negotiating with an urge at your weakest moment. If it takes real friction to get there — a device left in another room, a blocker in place, a plan for what else to do at that hour — you've already won half the fight before it starts.

This isn't about willpower at all. It's about making the harmful choice slightly harder to reach and the better choice slightly easier, so future-you isn't relying on in-the-moment strength that may not be there.

Look at your own patterns: what time, what place, what device, what's usually happening right before. Small, boring, structural changes in those spots tend to outperform sheer determination by a wide margin.
The most unglamorous tools are often the most effective ones.

Sleep deprivation and a sedentary day both raise stress levels and strain the same self-regulation you rely on when an urge hits. It's one of the most consistent, least exciting findings in the self-control research: rested, physically active people report fewer and weaker urges — not because they're more disciplined, but because their baseline stress is simply lower.

You don't need a training plan or a perfect sleep schedule to benefit from this. A short walk, a few push-ups, or getting to bed thirty minutes earlier all measurably shift the odds in your favor for the next day.

Think of sleep and movement as infrastructure sitting underneath every other tool in this app, not as separate goals of their own. When that infrastructure is solid, everything else gets easier by default.
Data, not a verdict on who you are.

A relapse can feel like proof that nothing has worked and nothing will. It isn't. It's one data point in a much longer pattern, and treating it as a final verdict is exactly what makes the next one more likely — shame is a worse motivator than it feels like it should be.

What matters far more than the relapse itself is what happens in the hour after it. Beating yourself up rarely changes behavior; it just adds pain on top of pain. Looking honestly at what led to it — what state you were in, what got skipped, what was within easy reach — actually changes something.

Every streak that's ever been rebuilt started the exact same way: from zero, right after a setback, by someone who kept going anyway. That's not a consolation story. It's the only way this has ever worked for anyone.
Being harsh with yourself doesn\'t make you more disciplined.

It's easy to assume that going easy on yourself after a setback is the same as letting yourself off the hook — that real accountability requires some amount of self-punishment. The research points the other way: people who treat themselves with basic kindness after a setback tend to recover faster and relapse less than people who respond with harsh self-criticism.

Self-compassion isn't pretending the setback didn't matter. It's talking to yourself the way you'd talk to a friend going through the same thing — honestly, but without contempt. Contempt doesn't build discipline. It just makes the whole subject something you want to avoid thinking about, which is the opposite of what recovery needs.

You can hold yourself to a real standard and still be kind to yourself when you miss it. Those aren't in conflict — they're what makes trying again next time possible at all.
Secrecy and loneliness feed the exact loop you\'re trying to change.

Habits people feel ashamed of tend to thrive in isolation. Secrecy removes the social friction that would normally interrupt a pattern — no one to notice, no one to check in with, nothing pulling attention outward. Loneliness is also one of the four H.A.L.T. states that reliably precedes an urge.

This is part of why reaching out, even briefly, is one of the more effective tools available — not because a conversation magically erases an urge, but because it interrupts the loop and reminds you there's a whole life happening outside of it.

It doesn't have to be a deep conversation about what you're going through. A text to a friend, a call home, five minutes talking about something completely unrelated — all of it counts, and all of it works against the isolation the habit depends on.
A ten-second habit that changes what an urge is offering you.

In the moment, an urge only shows you the next thirty seconds — relief, escape, a hit of something familiar. It never shows you the fifteen minutes after that: the same emptiness, plus regret this time, plus whatever streak or momentum you'd built now reset.

"Playing the tape forward" means deliberately imagining that whole sequence before acting, not just the appealing first frame. Ask yourself, honestly: how will I feel about this exact choice in twenty minutes? Most of the time, the honest answer is the same one it was yesterday.

This takes ten seconds and no special willpower — just a pause long enough to see the whole picture instead of the edited highlight reel the urge is offering. It's one of the simplest tools here, and one of the most reliable.
